additionalTime: 10 minutes potato steam-dry time, 10 minutes chile soak, plus 3 to 5 minutes foil rest
author: Robert Carr
description: A focused sausage, egg, potato, and cheese breakfast burrito with crispy Yukon Gold potatoes, deeply browned breakfast sausage, soft scrambled eggs, a three-cheese melt, toasted flour tortillas, and a simmered guajillo-arbol breakfast drip sauce served on the side for adding to each bite.
equipment: Large 12-inch heavy skillet or saute pan
Nonstick skillet for eggs, optional but helpful
ingredients: 10 ounces Yukon Gold potatoes, cut into 1/2-inch cubes
2 teaspoons apple cider vinegar for the potato water
2 teaspoons Diamond Crystal kosher salt for the potato water
1 1/2 teaspoons cornstarch
1/2 teaspoon smoked paprika
1/2 teaspoon garlic powder
1/4 teaspoon onion powder
1/2 teaspoon Diamond Crystal kosher salt for the potatoes, or 1/4 teaspoon Morton kosher salt
1/2 teaspoon black pepper for the potatoes
6 ounces bulk pork breakfast sausage, preferably sage-forward
1 tablespoon avocado oil for the potatoes, only as needed
4 large eggs
1 1/2 tablespoons half-and-half
1/4 teaspoon Diamond Crystal kosher salt for the eggs, or 1/8 teaspoon Morton kosher salt
1/4 teaspoon black pepper for the eggs
1 tablespoon unsalted butter
2 ounces sharp cheddar cheese, freshly shredded
2 ounces Monterey Jack cheese, freshly shredded
2 slices American cheese
2 large burrito-size flour tortillas, preferably 12 inches
1 dried guajillo chile, stemmed and seeded
1 dried chile de arbol pepper, stem removed
2 tomatillos, husked and rinsed
1 Roma tomato
1 jalapeno
1/8 white onion
2 garlic cloves, unpeeled
1 tablespoon apple cider vinegar for the hot sauce
2 teaspoons fresh lime juice for the hot sauce
1 tablespoon chile soaking water or fresh hot water, plus more as needed
2 teaspoons avocado oil for the hot sauce
1/2 teaspoon honey
1/2 teaspoon cumin for the hot sauce
1/2 teaspoon Diamond Crystal kosher salt for the hot sauce, or 1/4 teaspoon Morton kosher salt
1/2 teaspoon black pepper for the hot sauce
instructions: Place the 10 ounces diced Yukon Gold potatoes in a medium saucepan and cover them with cold water by 1 inch.
Add 2 teaspoons apple cider vinegar and 2 teaspoons Diamond Crystal kosher salt to the potato water.
Bring the potatoes to a boil over high heat, then reduce the heat and simmer for 5 to 7 minutes, until the potatoes are barely tender but not falling apart.
Drain the potatoes well, spread them on paper towels or a wire rack, pat the tops dry if they look wet, and let them steam-dry for 10 minutes.
While the potatoes dry, make the simmered guajillo-arbol breakfast drip sauce.

Toast 1 stemmed and seeded dried guajillo chile and 1 stemmed dried chile de arbol pepper in a dry large heavy skillet or saute pan, over medium heat for 15 to 30 seconds per side, turning often, just until fragrant and slightly softened, not scorched.
Transfer the toasted chiles to a small bowl, cover them with hot water, and soak for 10 minutes.
While the chiles soak, place 2 tomatillos, 1 Roma tomato, 1 jalapeno, 1/8 white onion, and 2 unpeeled garlic cloves in the same dry large heavy skillet or saute pan over medium-high heat.

Cook the hot sauce vegetables for 8 to 12 minutes, turning occasionally, until the tomatillos soften, the garlic is browned and soft inside, and the tomatoes, jalapenos, and onion are charred in places.
Peel the roasted garlic cloves.
Drain the soaked chiles, reserving the soaking water.
Taste the reserved chile soaking water. If it tastes bitter or harsh, use fresh hot water for blending and thinning instead.
Transfer the soaked chiles to a blender.

Add the roasted tomatillos, tomato, jalapeno, onion, peeled garlic, 1 tablespoon apple cider vinegar, 2 teaspoons fresh lime juice, 1 tablespoon chile soaking water or fresh hot water, 1/2 teaspoon honey, 1/2 teaspoon cumin, 1/2 teaspoon Diamond Crystal kosher salt, and 1/2 teaspoon black pepper to the blender.
Blend the hot sauce on high for 35 to 60 seconds until very smooth, adding 1 to 3 tablespoons more chile soaking water or fresh hot water only if needed to make it loose enough to drip from a spoon.
Heat 2 teaspoons avocado oil in a small saucepan over medium heat.
Carefully pour the blended hot sauce into the hot oil; it may splatter at first.
Simmer the hot sauce over medium-low heat for 5 to 7 minutes, stirring often, until the color deepens slightly, the oil looks integrated, and the sauce lightly coats a spoon but still drips easily.
If the hot sauce gets too thick, whisk in chile soaking water or fresh hot water 1 tablespoon at a time until it drips easily again.
Taste the hot sauce and adjust only if needed with a small pinch of kosher salt, a few drops of lime juice, or a few drops of honey.
For a smoother drip sauce, pass the hot sauce through a fine-mesh strainer; for a more rustic sauce, leave it unstrained.

Cook 6 ounces bulk pork breakfast sausage in a large skillet over medium heat for 6 to 8 minutes, breaking it into small crumbles and letting the crumbles sit against the pan long enough to brown, until deeply browned and fully cooked.
Transfer the sausage to a paper towel-lined plate with a slotted spoon.
Reserve 1 tablespoon of sausage fat in the skillet and discard the rest, or add 1 tablespoon avocado oil if the sausage did not release enough fat.

Toss the steam-dried potatoes with 1 1/2 teaspoons cornstarch, 1/2 teaspoon smoked paprika, 1/2 teaspoon garlic powder, 1/4 teaspoon onion powder, 1/2 teaspoon Diamond Crystal kosher salt, and 1/2 teaspoon black pepper.
Heat the reserved sausage fat in the skillet over medium-high heat until hot.
Add the seasoned potatoes in a single layer, using a second skillet or working in batches if needed so they are not crowded.
Cook the potatoes, or each batch, undisturbed for 3 minutes, then turn every 2 to 3 minutes for 10 to 14 minutes total, until deeply golden and crisp on multiple sides.
Transfer the crispy potatoes to a plate and keep them uncovered so they stay crisp.
Whisk 4 large eggs with 1 1/2 tablespoons half-and-half, 1/4 teaspoon Diamond Crystal kosher salt, and 1/4 teaspoon black pepper until no streaks of egg white remain.
Melt 1 tablespoon unsalted butter in a nonstick skillet over medium-low heat, or for minimum cookware, wipe out the large heavy skillet or saute pan, let it cool to medium-low, and use that instead.
Add the eggs and cook gently, folding with a rubber spatula, until the eggs form soft curds and are just set but still slightly looser than you want in the finished burrito.
Immediately transfer the eggs to a plate and spread them into a shallow layer for 1 to 2 minutes so leftover heat slows down and excess steam escapes.
Warm each 12-inch flour tortilla in a dry skillet for 20 to 30 seconds per side, or until soft, flexible, and lightly toasted in spots.
Assemble 1 burrito at a time by placing a warm tortilla on a clean work surface.
Place 1 slice American cheese just below the center of the tortilla.
Sprinkle about 1 ounce sharp cheddar cheese and 1 ounce Monterey Jack cheese over the American cheese.
Add half of the crispy potatoes over the cheese.
Add half of the browned breakfast sausage over the potatoes.
Add half of the soft scrambled eggs over the sausage.
Fold the left and right sides of the tortilla over the filling, then roll tightly from the bottom into a compact burrito.
Place the burrito seam-side down in a dry skillet over medium heat.
Toast the burrito for 1 to 2 minutes per side, starting with the seam side, until lightly crisp, sealed, and browned in spots.
Wrap the toasted burrito in foil and let it rest for 3 to 5 minutes so the cheese finishes melting and the filling settles.
Repeat with the remaining tortillas and fillings.
Serve each burrito with the simmered guajillo-arbol breakfast drip sauce on the side.
Drip a small spoonful of hot sauce onto each bite as you eat instead of pouring it inside the burrito.
notes: The minimum cookware setup is 1 medium saucepan for the potatoes, 1 small saucepan for the hot sauce, and 1 large 12-inch heavy skillet or saute pan for the chiles, roasted sauce vegetables, sausage, potatoes, tortillas, and burrito toasting.

A separate nonstick skillet for the eggs is optional and makes the workflow easier, but it is not required. If using the large stainless skillet for eggs, wipe it clean after the potatoes, let it cool to medium-low, use the full 1 tablespoon butter, and fold the eggs gently with a rubber spatula.
This recipe intentionally keeps the burrito filling limited to sausage, egg, potato, and cheese. The hot sauce is served outside the burrito and does not count as an internal filling.

The reduced filling ratio is intentional. Two burritos made with 10 ounces potatoes, 6 ounces sausage, 4 eggs, and the listed cheese are still full, but they roll cleaner and eat with better balance than an overstuffed version.
Bulk pork breakfast sausage is better than sliced sausage links because it browns into small crumbles that distribute evenly through the burrito.
A sage-forward breakfast sausage gives the most classic breakfast flavor. Avoid maple breakfast sausage unless you specifically want a sweeter burrito.
Reserve only enough sausage fat to crisp the potatoes. Too much loose grease makes the burrito heavy.
Yukon Gold potatoes are the best choice because they get creamy inside and crisp outside without turning mealy.
The vinegar in the potato water helps the potato cubes hold their shape while they simmer.
The cornstarch coating is a small but important crispness boost. It helps the potatoes brown and gives them a rough, crisp exterior.
The potatoes should be crisp before they go into the burrito because they soften slightly after the foil rest.
Do not crowd the potatoes in the skillet. A crowded pan steams the potato cubes and prevents the rough cornstarch-coated edges from turning crisp.
Do not overcook the eggs until dry. The target is soft, tender curds with no wet liquid pooling in the pan.
Spreading the eggs on a plate before assembly prevents leftover heat from making them dry and lets extra steam escape before the burrito is wrapped.
Freshly shredded cheddar and Monterey Jack melt better than pre-shredded cheese. The American cheese is included for melt, binding, and burrito-shop texture.
Use large 12-inch flour tortillas. Smaller tortillas will be difficult to roll with this much filling.
Do not overfill the burritos. A tightly rolled slightly smaller burrito eats better than a huge torn one.
Toast the assembled burrito seam-side down first. It seals the fold, melts the cheese, and gives the tortilla a lightly crisp exterior.
The foil rest matters. It helps the cheese fully melt, lets the burrito hold together, and gives the tortilla a better breakfast-shop texture.
Keep the hot sauce on the side if you want the tortilla to stay firmer. Drip it onto each bite while eating.
The guajillo chiles give the sauce body, color, and deeper red chile flavor without making it aggressively hot. The chile de arbol peppers provide the sharper heat.
Do not use scorched dried chiles. A few dark spots on roasted vegetables are good, but blackened dried chiles can make the sauce bitter.
If the chile soaking water tastes bitter, do not use it. Fresh hot water gives the sauce a cleaner finished flavor.
Simmering the blended hot sauce in oil makes it taste more finished, less raw, and more like a burrito-shop sauce.
For a milder hot sauce, remove the jalapeno seeds and use 1 dried chile de arbol pepper instead of 2.
For a hotter hot sauce, use 4 to 5 dried chile de arbol peppers or add 1 serrano.
The hot sauce should be loose enough to drip but not watery. Add chile soaking water or fresh hot water 1 tablespoon at a time only after blending or simmering.
These burritos are best eaten immediately after the foil rest, but leftovers can be wrapped tightly in foil and reheated in a 350 degrees F oven until hot.
title: Sausage, Egg, Potato, and Cheese Breakfast Burritos with Simmered Guajillo-Arbol Breakfast Drip Sauce
